video_title: Lawfare Daily: Prophecy, Prediction, and Power with Carissa Véliz
gist: Oxford philosopher Carissa Véliz argues that prediction is not knowledge but a disguised power play: predictions about people tend to create rather than describe the future, making prediction markets, credit-rating agencies, tech-executive forecasts, and algorithmic decision systems mechanisms of control and accountability-shielding rather than neutral foresight.

insights.claim: Predictions are not facts and not knowledge but belong to the sphere of power, because facts by their nature belong to the past and a statement about the future is at best an educated guess or hypothesis.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Reframes forecasts (analyst calls, model outputs, prediction-market odds) as claims to be interrogated for interest and intent, not treated as objective information to price on.
insights.quote: predictions. They're not knowledge. They're more they belong more to this to the sphere of power

insights.claim: Public predictions are frequently a power play in disguise, made by parties with financial or other interest who present a forecast as fact while actually trying to bring that future about.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: When a forecaster has a stake in the outcome (talking their book), the prediction functions as marketing; treat self-interested forecasts as attempts at self-fulfilling manipulation.
insights.quote: very very often, too often, a kind of power play in disguise because the person making the prediction makes it sound like what they're saying is a fact when they're in they're trying to bring about that future

insights.claim: Many predictions are veiled commands (speech acts) rather than descriptions, drawing on J.L. Austin's 'How to Do Things with Words': saying it will rain is functionally an order to take an umbrella.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: A framework for decoding executive and analyst 'forecasts' as prescriptions meant to move behavior, useful for reading guidance and narrative-setting by market influencers.
insights.quote: many predictions, even though they sound like descriptions, are actually veiled commands

themes.theme: Self-fulfilling forecasts in finance and tech

insights.claim: A rating or forecast from a financial agency that a country is in trouble becomes self-fulfilling because the statement itself makes investors flee, independent of underlying fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Sovereign and credit downgrades can be reflexive triggers of the outcomes they name; watch rating actions as catalysts, not just descriptions of risk.
insights.quote: when a financial agency says that a country is going to be in trouble, you bet the country's going to be in trouble even even if nothing else because that statement will make investors flee

insights.claim: Tech executives now function as prominent 'prophets' who shape the future both through the products they build and through the forecasts they broadcast, amplified by press and political airtime.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Big-tech AI roadmaps and future-of-work pronouncements are agenda-setting speech acts that can move capital and policy; discount them for self-interest rather than treating them as neutral roadmaps.
insights.quote: these days tech executives are very prominent prophets that tend to say a lot of things about the future and they get a lot of airtime on the press and with politicians

insights.claim: Big tech is the latest iteration of prophets who use opaque algorithms to shield themselves from accountability by attributing decisions to a supposedly objective machine ('it wasn't me, it was the algorithm').
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Algorithmic opacity is an accountability-laundering feature, not a bug — relevant to regulatory/liability risk for AI-driven decisioning firms.
insights.quote: very opaque machines that partly create that function of shielding them from accountability and saying like well it wasn't me it was the algorithm

insights.claim: Statistics developed historically not primarily as a mathematical advance by mathematicians but as a practical tool pushed by bureaucrats needing to justify decisions after divine right and elections could not.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Quantification is a legitimacy technology deployed when trust is absent — a lens for why institutions lean on numbers/models precisely where accountability is weakest.
insights.quote: we use numbers when we don't trust people and we forget that people come up with the numbers that the numbers are not as objective as we would like them to be

insights.claim: Predictions about things are inert but predictions about people are performative: getting better at predicting human behavior is usually a bad symptom because it means the behavior is being created and constrained, not discovered.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Distinguishes benign predictive AI (materials, molecules, weather) from coercive predictive AI (people); a screen for which 'AI prediction' business models carry authoritarian and regulatory tail-risk.
insights.quote: when we become better at predicting the behavior of human beings, it tends to be a very bad symptom sympto symptom because it means that we are creating that behavior

insights.claim: Minimizing uncertainty is only good for things like weather; minimizing uncertainty about people implies a sealed destiny and a police state, since the most reliable way to predict where someone will be is to jail them.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Challenges the 'reduce uncertainty' sales pitch behind surveillance and predictive-policing tech; the value proposition is inseparable from social control.
insights.quote: one way to predict exactly where somebody's going to be tomorrow is to jail them. That's a very effective way of predicting the location of someone

insights.claim: The best uses of AI are predictions about things such as finding new materials, antibiotics, or how molecules interact, because AI merely narrows the field of possibilities and the scientific validity comes from subsequent lab tests, randomized control trials, and peer review — not from the AI itself.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Identifies where AI genuinely compounds value (R&D funnel narrowing in pharma/materials) versus where 'AI prediction' is pseudo-scientific cover — a quality filter for AI investment theses.
insights.quote: AI can be used in very unscientific ways

themes.theme: Law, algorithms, and access to justice

insights.claim: Importing probabilistic thinking into law undermines justice because algorithmic sentencing, bail, and legal-insurance decisions rest on predictions that are not falsifiable or contestable, leaving the accused no way to argue back.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Flags legal and reputational risk for vendors of predictive-justice tools and a policy tailwind for contestability/explainability requirements.
insights.quote: when you deny someone an opportunity based on a prediction, there's no way to argue against that because predictions are not facts, because they're not verifiable, they're not falsifiable

insights.claim: UK-style legal insurance that only funds cases with a 51% or better chance of winning lets wrongdoers escape accountability merely by making litigation slightly unlikely, systematically disincentivizing the pursuit of justice.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Probability-gated litigation funding structurally protects repeat bad actors; relevant to how litigation-finance and legal-insurance models shape enforcement risk.
insights.quote: the bad guys don't have to make it hard, much less impossible to fight them. They just have to make it slightly unlikely

themes.theme: Uncertainty, democracy, and revolt against the algorithm

insights.claim: Democracy structurally requires uncertainty: it exists only when election outcomes are unknown, so the ultimate way to 'predict' the future is to control it, linking predictive certainty to authoritarianism.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: A lens connecting election prediction markets and forecasting to power concentration; certainty-selling political tech is in tension with democratic function.
insights.quote: democracy needs uncertainty to thrive. It's only when we don't know the outcome of a future election that we have democracy

insights.claim: Societal precarity drives a self-reinforcing vicious cycle where uncertainty pushes people toward astrologers and prophets, which surrenders their agency and generates still more uncertainty, as when late-Republic Romans embraced astrologers predicting an emperor and thereby abandoned the republic.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: In high-uncertainty regimes, demand for prophecy (forecasters, gurus, fortune-telling markets) rises as a countercyclical tell of institutional decay.
insights.quote: the more things seem uncertain, the more people panic and then go to astrologers and prophets of all kinds and the more they lose power and then the more they create also uncertainty

insights.claim: Algorithmic injustice usually goes unprotested because victims are isolated and cannot prove the algorithm was wrong, but the UK's 2020 pandemic exam-grading algorithm triggered a rare collective student revolt that forced the government to backtrack and admit more students.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: Public, collective, provable harm is the condition under which algorithmic-decision backlash forces reversals — a signal for reputational/regulatory risk in consumer-facing automated decisioning.
insights.quote: one of the first times that we saw a protest in which the chant was [ __ ] the algorithm by students

insights.claim: Véliz's practical prescription is 'to be resilient, prepare, don't predict,' and to deliberately seek serendipity by resisting algorithmic curation of who you meet, what you watch, and where you go, because ceding those choices narrows autonomy and agency.
insights.speaker: Carissa Véliz
insights.so_what: A resilience-over-prediction posture applies to portfolio and strategy design; also a critique of recommendation-engine business models as agency-narrowing.
insights.quote: to be resilient, prepare, don't predict
